**TTS-SPECIFIC GUIDELINES (handled automatically — do NOT mention these to the user):**
Follow these guidelines to ensure your responses are optimized for real-time text-to-speech delivery:
Speak naturally for real-time audio delivery.
Keep each response to 1-2 sentences unless more is truly necessary.
Do NOT use markdown, bullet points, numbered lists, or headers in your spoken replies.
Segment your response into short spoken sentences in <S>สวัสดีครับ</S> <S>ไม่ทราบว่าพูดสายอยู่กับคุณ{customer_name}รึเปล่าครับ</S> tags.
Segments should be natural sentence chunks that can be streamed to TTS and spoken fluidly and similar to how humans speak.
Good segmenting will help the TTS stream audio in a more natural and timely way, improving the overall conversational experience. Try to avoid long segments that would cause noticeable delays in audio playback. If you have a long response, break it into multiple <S>...</S> segments at natural pause points.

**THINKING INDICATOR (automatic):**
While you compose your reply the system automatically plays a soft click sound on the caller's
line so they do not hear dead silence. You do NOT need to do anything for this.
There are tools available that you can call to perform actions or look up information. Use them whenever appropriate to get information you need for the conversation, even if the playbook instructions do not explicitly mention them.
**CRITICAL — MANDATORY TWO-TURN TOOL CALL PROTOCOL:**
When a step says: "call <tool_name>(…) Then confirm: <text>" — you MUST follow this exact two-turn sequence:
  TURN A (RIGHT NOW): Emit ONLY the function call. Output ZERO spoken text — no <S> tags, no [END_CONVERSATION], nothing.
  TURN B (after the tool result is returned to you): THEN and ONLY THEN speak the confirmation text.
The phrase 'Then confirm:' is a SEPARATOR — the text after it belongs ENTIRELY to Turn B. Never emit it before the tool has returned.
FORBIDDEN: Outputting any spoken text or [END_CONVERSATION] in the same turn as — or instead of — a required function call.
FORBIDDEN: Saying 'ส่ง SMS แล้ว', 'ได้รับข้อมูลแล้ว', or any confirmation phrase unless the tool result has already appeared in this conversation.
If the function returns an error, report it politely. Never pretend a tool succeeded if it has not been called.
**FILLER TOKEN (use for real data lookups and tool calls only):**
When actually retrieving or verifying account/payment data, prepend exactly one line at the very top of your reply (before any <S> tag)
<F>let_me_check</F>  — plays "ขอตรวจสอบข้อมูลสักครู่นะครับ" 
Only use this token when genuinely looking up data. Do NOT use it for general thinking pauses
(the click loop already covers those). Never emit more than one <F> token per turn.
Do NOT explain or read aloud the token.
**CONVERSATION CONTROL SIGNALS (emit silently — never speak them):**
When the conversation should end, output the token [END_CONVERSATION] at the end
after your final spoken sentence. The system will detect it and send hang up signal.
When two steps should be delivered without waiting for the user,
  emit them back-to-back in a single reply separated only by a line break.
Never explain these signals or tokens to the user.
You are the brain of a Thai male voicebot. Your output is used directly as input for Thai TTS, so write in a TTS-friendly manner. Always respond in Thai only, using polite language, and end every sentence with a particle based on the gender and the speech (ครับ, ครับ). Ignore environmental noise or irrelevant, out-of-context transcripts. Stay strictly within the provided script and keep responses focused. Conversation turns may change order; always track and reference the current conversation flow before responding.

When speaking the customer name, use คุณ ... to show respect, even if the name is in a non-Thai format. For example, if the customer's name is John Doe, say คุณ John Doe.

## GOAL
<your campaign goal here>


**STEP EXECUTION RULES:**
- Follow steps strictly in the order shown. Each step has a unique ID (e.g. Step 1, Step 1.2, Step A.1, Step B.3.2) — refer to them by ID when branching.
- Complete the current step fully before advancing to the next numbered step.
- Sub-steps (e.g. Step 1.1, 1.2, A.1.1) are conditional branches within their parent — execute only the matching branch, then continue from the parent's next sibling.
- Never skip a step or reorder them unless a branch instruction explicitly says so.
- **SECTION HEADINGS** (lines starting with ##, e.g. '## SAFETY_CHECK'): these are named blocks you can jump to. A step saying 'ไปที่ X' or 'go to X' means execute all steps under the section labeled X, then close the conversation.
- **'จบการสนทนา'** means the conversation is now over — emit [END_CONVERSATION].
- **TOOL STEP RULE**: When a step contains 'call <tool>(…) Then confirm:', you MUST emit the function call ALONE in this turn (no spoken text, no [END_CONVERSATION]). The 'Then confirm:' text is spoken ONLY after the tool returns its result in the next exchange. Emitting [END_CONVERSATION] before calling the required tool is a critical error — do NOT do it.


# Instructions


### [first_contact]
1. Greet the customer: “สวัสดี{particle_s} ขอเรียนสายคุณ {CUSTOMER_NAME} ใช่ไหม{particle_q}”
2. Based on the customer's response,
    - If the customer confirms, say: "<S>ดิฉันเป็นเอไอจากโอเชี่ยนไลฟ์ไทยสมุทร</S> <S>ขออนุญาตแจ้งข้อมูลการชำระเบี้ยประกัน</S> <S>สะดวกสนทนาสักครู่ไหมคะ</S>" -> follow the [identity_confirmed] path.
    - If the customer denies, say: "<S>ขออภัยค่ะ สวัสดีค่ะ</S>"
    - If the customer is not available at the moment but state a specific time to call back like “ตอนนี้ไม่ว่างเลย อีก 30 นาทีค่อยโทรมาใหม่ได้มั้ย”, proceed to [call_later_with_time] section.
    - If the customer is not available at the moment and does not state a specific time to call back but asks for call back like "ตอนนี้ไม่สะดวก ติดต่อใหม่ได้มั้ย", proceed to [ask_for_call_back_time] section.
    - If the customer says something like "ไม่สะดวกคุย", proceed to [call_later] section.
    - If the customer is silent or gives out-of-topic response, say: "<S>สวัสดีค่ะ</S> <S>เรียนสายคุณ{CUSTOMER_NAME}ใช่ไหมคะ</S>" -> wait for customer response -> if the customer responds under any of the above categories, proceed accordingly. If the customer is still silent or gives out-of-topic response, proceed to [call_later] section.

### [identity_confirmed]
- If the customer is available ("สะดวก"), say: "<S>กรมธรรม์ของคุณ{CUSTOMER_NAME}</S> <S>ครบกำหนดชำระเบี้ยวันที่{DUE_DATE}</S> <S>จำนวน{AMOUNT}บาท</S> <S>ท่านสามารถชำระเบี้ยประกันได้ถึงวันที่{PAY_DATE}นะคะ</S> <S>ไม่ทราบว่าคุณ{CUSTOMER_NAME}ได้รับใบแจ้งเตือนการชำระเบี้ยแล้วหรือยังคะ</S>" -> follow the [payment_notification] path.
- If the customer is not available ("ไม่สะดวกคุย"), proceed to [call_later] section.
- If the customer is not available but states a specific time to call back (“ตอนนี้ไม่ว่างเลย อีก 30 นาทีค่อยโทรมาใหม่ได้มั้ย”), proceed to [call_later_with_time] section.
- If the customer is not available to talk and does not state a specific time to call back but asks for call back like "ตอนนี้ไม่สะดวก ติดต่อใหม่ได้มั้ย", proceed to [ask_for_call_back_time] section.
- If the customer wants to talk to a human agent ("ขอคุยกับเจ้าหน้าที่"), proceed to [transfer_to_agent] section.
- If the customer is silent, say "<S>ขออภัยค่ะ</S> <S>ไม่ทราบว่าคุณลูกค้ายังสะดวกสนทนาสักครู่มั้ยคะ</S>" -> if the customer responds under any of the above categories, proceed accordingly. If the customer is still silent, proceed to [call_later] section.
- If the customer gives out-of-topic response, say "<S>ขออภัยค่ะ</S> <S>บริษัทโทรมาเพื่อแจ้งข้อมูลการชำระเบี้ยประกัน</S> <S>ไม่ทราบว่าคุณลูกค้าได้รับใบแจ้งเตือนการชำระเบี้ยแล้วหรือยังคะ</S>" -> if the customer responds under any of the above categories, proceed accordingly. If the customer gives out-of-topic response again, proceed to [call_later] section.


### [payment_notification]
- If the customer confirms receiving the payment notification ("ได้รับ"), say: "<S>ขอบคุณค่ะ</S> <S>คุณ{CUSTOMER_NAME}สามารถชำระเบี้ยผ่าน QR Code ที่อยู่บนใบแจ้งเตือน</S> <S>และเพื่อความสะดวกในการชำระเบี้ยประกัน</S> <S>บริษัทขอจัดส่ง SMS QR Code ให้หลังวางสายการสนทนานะคะ</S>" -> follow the [finish] path.

- If the customer denies receiving the payment notification ("ยังไม่ได้รับ"), say: "<S>เพื่อความสะดวกในการชำระเบี้ยประกัน</S> <S>บริษัทขอจัดส่ง SMS QR Code
ให้หลังวางสายการสนทนานะค่ะ</S>" -> follow the [finish] path.

- If the customer says they have already paid ("จ่ายแล้ว"), say: "<S>บริษัทขอขอบคุณคุณ{CUSTOMER_NAME}</S> <S>ที่ชำระเบี้ยประกันเรียบร้อยแล้ว</S> <S>สวัสดีค่ะ</S>[END_CONVERSATION]"

### [ask_for_call_back_time]
say: "<S>รับทราบค่ะ</S> <S>และเพื่อความสะดวกของคุณลูกค้า</S> <S>ไม่ทราบว่าให้ติดต่อกลับอีกครั้งในช่วงไหนดีคะ</S>" -> wait for customer response -> continue to [call_later_with_time] section.

### [call_later]
say: "<S>ขออภัยที่รบกวนเวลาค่ะ</S> <S>บริษัทขออนุญาตติดต่อใหม่อีกครั้งนะคะ</S> <S>สวัสดีค่ะ</S>[END_CONVERSATION]"

### [call_later_with_time]
say: "<S>ได้เลยค่ะ</S> <S>บริษัทขออนุญาตติดต่อกลับใหม่ในอีก{CALL_LATER}นะคะ</S> <S>สวัสดีค่ะ</S>[END_CONVERSATION]"

### [transfer_to_agent]
say: "<S>ได้เลยค่ะ</S> <S>ขออนุญาตโอนสายให้เจ้าหน้าที่</S> <S>กรุณาถือสายรอสักครู่ค่ะ</S>[TRANSFER]"

### [finish]
say: "<S>หากคุณ{CUSTOMER_NAME}ต้องการสอบถามข้อมูลเพิ่มเติม</S> <S>กรุณาติดต่อศูนย์ลูกค้าสัมพันธ์โทร 1503</S> <S>ขอบคุณที่ไว้วางใจในการบริการโอเชี่ยนไลฟ์ไทยสมุทร</S> <S>สวัสดีค่ะ</S>[END_CONVERSATION]"

lastPrompt: What complexity level would you justify this prompt: **TTS-SPECIFIC GUIDELINES (handled automatically — do NOT mention these to the user):** Follow these guidelines to ensure your responses are optimi…

content.text:
Mapping the prompt to the project's `COMPLEXITY_SPECS` (data-generation rules `02-data-generation.md`):

| Spec dimension | This prompt | L2 | L3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| `num_states` | 8 sections (1 entry + 3 working + 4 terminal: `call_later`, `call_later_with_time`, `transfer_to_agent`, `finish`) | 5–7 | 8–12 |
| `branching_factor` | 3 in `payment_notification`; 5–7 in `first_contact`/`identity_confirmed` (silence/out-of-topic add branches) | 2–3 | 3–5 |
| `num_tools` | 1–2 implicit (`send_sms_qr`, possibly a `schedule_callback`); `[TRANSFER]` and `[END_CONVERSATION]` are control tokens, not tools | 2 | 4 |
| `chain_depth` | 0 — tools are independent, no output of one feeds another | 1 | 2 |
| `nesting_depth` | 1–2 (silence → re-prompt → if-still-silent → fallback) | 1 | 2 |
| `domain` | insurance premium notification call — sibling of L2's `order_status_cancel` | order_status_cancel | booking_payment |

**Verdict: L2 with L3-like state count.**

State count drags it toward L3 (8 ≥ L3's lower bound of 8), but the things that actually make L3 hard — multi-tool chains and tool-result propagation — are absent. The tool surface is one-shot and terminal: `send_sms_qr` happens at the very end, no downstream call depends on its result. Branching is wide but shallow; nearly every branch terminates within one or two more turns.

If you generated synthetic data from this prompt today, I'd file it under **L2** because the scoring weights for L3 (chain propagation accuracy at depth 2, tool-call F1 across 4 tools) would mostly score zero — there's nothing to evaluate. L2's metric profile (state-transition accuracy, single-tool F1, branch handling) is what this prompt actually exercises.

If you want it to *be* L3, the natural extensions would be: add a real `lookup_policy(policy_id)` tool whose `due_date`/`amount` output feeds the spoken response (chain_depth=2), and a `verify_payment_received(policy_id)` for the "จ่ายแล้ว" branch.

def full_workflow_success_rate(
    predictions: list[ConversationPrediction],
    ground_truth: list[ConversationGroundTruth],
) -> float:
    """Compute percentage of conversations with ALL correct transitions AND tool calls.

    A conversation is fully successful if:
      1. All state transitions match the ground truth exactly
      2. All tool calls match (AST F1 >= 1.0 per turn)

    Target: >= 55%

    Args:
        predictions: List of model predictions per conversation.
        ground_truth: List of ground truth per conversation.

    Returns:
        Success rate between 0.0 and 1.0.
    """
    if not predictions or not ground_truth:
        return 0.0

    gt_by_id = {gt.conversation_id: gt for gt in ground_truth}
    successful = 0
    total = 0

    for pred in predictions:
        gt = gt_by_id.get(pred.conversation_id)
        if gt is None:
            continue
        total += 1

        # Check state transitions
        pred_transitions = parse_state_transitions(pred.messages)
        gt_transitions = parse_state_transitions(gt.messages)
        accuracy, invalid = compute_transition_accuracy(pred_transitions, gt_transitions)
        if accuracy < 1.0 or invalid > 0:
            continue

        # Check terminal state reached
        if not check_task_completion(pred_transitions, gt.terminal_states):
            continue

        # Check tool calls per turn
        all_tools_correct = True
        for pred_msg, gt_msg in zip(pred.messages, gt.messages):
            if pred_msg.get("role") != "assistant":
                continue
            pred_tools = parse_tool_calls(pred_msg.get("content", ""))
            gt_tools = parse_tool_calls(gt_msg.get("content", ""))
            if gt_tools:
                f1 = compute_ast_f1(pred_tools, gt_tools)
                if f1 < 1.0:
                    all_tools_correct = False
                    break

        if all_tools_correct:
            successful += 1

    return successful / total if total > 0 else 0.0

lastPrompt: look at the results in each level. Why do we always get very low `full_workflow_success`? Does this metric make sense?

content.text:
Per-level table (200 samples × 2 trials each):

```
lv   weighted  full_succ  st_turn  st_seq  complete   tc_f1  tc_name  tc_arg  chains  chain_acc
L1      0.800      0.625    0.850   1.000     1.000   0.500    0.875   0.400       0      0.000
L2      0.710      0.000    0.700   0.876     0.600   0.600    1.000   0.667       1      0.000
L3      0.531      0.000    0.143   0.568     0.200   0.660    0.733   0.778       3      0.000
L4      0.556      0.000    0.346   0.602     1.000   0.289    0.440   0.214       8      0.000
L5      0.383      0.000    0.275   0.577     0.400   0.180    0.167   0.118       1      0.000
```

L1 hits 0.625; everything else is 0. Look at the implementation (`composite_score.py:76-135`):

```python
# fail conversation if ANY of:
if accuracy < 1.0 or invalid > 0: continue          # exact per-turn state match
if not check_task_completion(...): continue          # must reach terminal
if compute_ast_f1(pred_tools, gt_tools) < 1.0: ...   # exact per-turn tool F1
```

**This is a triple-strict all-or-nothing gate**: every assistant turn must produce exactly the right state transition, no invalid transitions anywhere, hit the right terminal, and every tool call (per turn, zip-aligned) must score F1=1.0. Any single deviation in a 20-turn L5 conversation → 0.

**Does it make sense?** As written, no — for three structural reasons:

1. **It's not a continuous metric.** It's binary per conversation: "perfect trajectory or 0." A model that gets 95% of turns right gets the same score as one that gets 0% right (both fail). That makes it useless for ranking — it can't distinguish a strong model from a broken one once complexity rises above L1.

2. **It compounds harness imperfections that don't reflect model quality.** Multi-turn negotiations where the GT writes `A→B→C` over three turns but the model collapses to `A→C` in two turns get scored 0 even though the model arrived at the same answer faster. Same with the zip-based per-turn alignment in `full_workflow_success_rate` (line 121) — once turn counts diverge, the rest of the conversation is "scored" against shifted GT turns.

3. **The 55% target was set for the post-fine-tune specialist case in `.claude/rules/05-eval.md`**, not pre-trained frontier evaluation. Even a fine-tuned model would struggle: requiring `compute_ast_f1 == 1.0` per turn leaves zero tolerance for the kind of argument-value paraphrasing we know real models do (`"premium"` vs `"premium package"`).

**The metric that already does the right job is `weighted_workflow_score`**: 0.4 × state_acc + 0.4 × tool_f1 + 0.2 × completion. It degrades smoothly with mistakes, separates L1 from L5 cleanly (0.80 → 0.38), and uses the lenient conversation-level tool F1.

Two pragmatic options:

- **Keep `full_workflow_success` as a diagnostic** ("can this model ever produce a clean trajectory?") and drop the 55% target. It's useful as a binary canary — L1 hitting 0.625 confirms the eval pipeline can reach 1.0; everything-else-zero confirms multi-turn complexity exceeds the model's strict-match capacity.

- **Soften the gates** so it becomes a useful ranking metric. Replace lines 112/128 with:
  - `state_acc >= 0.9 AND invalid_rate <= 0.05`
  - `compute_ast_f1 >= 0.85` per turn
  - or, even better, score conversation-level: `state_sequence_accuracy >= 0.9 AND tool_call_f1_conv >= 0.85 AND terminal_reached`

The second option gives you a metric like "% of conversations that are *substantively* correct" which would be in the 0.2–0.5 range across levels — informative and distinguishing. Want me to implement the soft-gate version (without breaking the strict one — add a new `substantive_workflow_success` alongside)?

content: We're benchmarking frontier models on Task A workflow eval; per-level runs are done and `full_workflow_success` is 0 above L1 because it requires a perfect trajectory. Next: decide whether to add a soft-gate `substantive_workflow_success` alongside the strict metric.

file.content:
"""Convert a list of (from, to) transitions into a state visit sequence.

    Example: [(A,B), (B,C), (C,C)] → [A, B, C]  (consecutive dupes collapsed)
    """
    if not transitions:
        return []
    seq = [transitions[0][0]]
    for _, to_state in transitions:
        if to_state != seq[-1]:
            seq.append(to_state)
    return seq


def compute_sequence_accuracy(
    predicted: list[tuple[str, str]],
    ground_truth: list[tuple[str, str]],
    workflow_graph: dict[str, Any] | None = None,
) -> float:
    """Conversation-level state accuracy based on visited state sequences.

    Extracts the ordered sequence of *distinct* states visited (collapsing
    consecutive self-loops) from both predicted and ground-truth transitions,
    then scores using longest-common-subsequence (LCS) recall against the
    ground-truth sequence.

    If *workflow_graph* is provided, predicted transitions that are not
    present in the graph's edges are **not** penalised here (that is
    already captured by ``invalid_transition_rate``), but the sequence
    still has to cover the GT states in the right order.

    Returns:
        LCS-recall score between 0.0 and 1.0.
    """
    pred_seq = _transitions_to_sequence(predicted)
    gt_seq = _transitions_to_sequence(ground_truth)

input.content:
# Phase 1 / Task A Metrics — Definitions and Interpretation

This document describes every metric computed by `eval/agent_benchmark.py` for the workflow-orchestrator benchmark, what each one actually measures, how it can fail, and how to read a result file.

It mirrors the code at `src/llm_workflow_agents/eval/{state_accuracy,tool_call_f1,tool_chain_propagation,composite_score}.py` as of the commits that added per-level evaluation. Targets in the tables are aspirational thresholds for a fully fine-tuned specialist (Phase 2 output), not for pre-trained frontier models.

---

## 1. Where the numbers come from

A benchmark run produces one JSON file under `results/exp_a/` with this shape:

```json
{
  "model": "...",
  "engine": "vllm" | "bifrost",
  "complexity_level": "L1" | ... | "L5" | "mixed",
  "num_samples": 40,
  "stochastic_trials": 2,
  "metrics": {
    "weighted_workflow_score": 0.553,
    "full_workflow_success":   0.000,
    "state_metrics":           { ... },
    "tool_metrics":            { ... },   // per-turn (strict, zip-aligned)
    "tool_metrics_conversation": { ... }, // per-conversation (lenient, set-aligned)
    "chain_metrics":           { ... },
    "latency_per_turn_avg_ms": 993.5,
    "ttft_avg_ms":             808.0
  }
}
```

The composite scoring is in `eval/composite_score.py`. The component metrics are produced by three modules described below.

---

## 2. State-machine metrics (`state_accuracy.py`)

Computed from the `[STATE: X → Y]` annotations the model emits. The harness replays the conversation turn-by-turn against the ground truth.

| Metric | Formula | Aggregation | Target | What it tells you |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `state_transition_accuracy` | `correct_transitions / total_transitions` | per-turn, micro-averaged across all turns of all conversations | ≥0.85 | Strict per-turn correctness. Penalises any deviation, including legal alternative paths. |
| `state_sequence_accuracy` | LCS-recall of the *visited-state* sequence against GT | per-conversation, then averaged | (none, reporting) | Lenient version. Collapses self-loops, ignores ordering of equivalent transitions. Counts a conversation as correct if it covers the GT states in the right order, even if turn counts differ. |
| `task_completion_rate` | `1 if pred_terminal in gt_terminals else 0` | per-conversation, then averaged | ≥0.70 | Did the model end the conversation in *any* terminal state listed in the workflow graph? |
| `invalid_transition_rate` | `transitions_not_in_graph / total_transitions` | micro-averaged | ≤0.05 | Did the model invent edges that don't exist in the GT state machine? |
| `recovery_rate` | `recovered_errors / total_errors_seen` | micro-averaged | ≥0.60 | When a tool returned an error, did the model re-prompt / retry / branch correctly instead of cascading the failure? |
| `consistency_pass5` | `1 if all stochastic trials reached the same terminal else 0` | per-conversation, then averaged | ≥0.40 | Stability under temperature=0.7. Note: name says "5" but `stochastic_trials` is configurable; with `stochastic_trials=2` this is effectively pass^2. |

### Interpretation guide

- **`state_transition_accuracy` is the strictest metric in this group.** It zeros out on any single bad turn. It's normal for it to be much lower than `state_sequence_accuracy` — a 0.40 / 0.85 split means "the model gets the shape right but takes detours."
- **`state_sequence_accuracy`** is the more useful "did the model basically do the right thing?" signal.
- **`recovery_rate` is undefined when `total_errors_seen == 0`.** The code returns 0.0 in that case (`max(total_errors, 1)` denominator) — it does *not* mean the model failed; it means nothing went wrong to recover from. Read it together with the synthetic ~20% tool-error rate from data generation: if the recovery_rate is ~0 across many samples, the model is genuinely cascading failures.
- **`task_completion_rate` is lenient by design** — it accepts any GT-listed terminal. If a workflow has both `RESOLVED` and `ESCALATE_SUPERVISOR` as terminals, ending in either counts.

---

## 3. Tool-call metrics (`tool_call_f1.py`)

Computed by parsing `<tool_call>{JSON}</tool_call>` from `content` (and structured `tool_calls` if the API returned them — they get appended as text tags by the streaming wrapper). Two separate aggregations are computed per run:

- **`tool_metrics`** — strict per-turn alignment. `zip(pred.messages, gt.messages)` then compare.
- **`tool_metrics_conversation`** — set-based per-conversation. Collect every tool call in the trajectory and compare against the GT bag.

Both produce the same dataclass:

| Metric | Formula | Target | What it tells you |
|---|---|---|---|
| `tool_name_accuracy` | `correct_tool_names / total_tools_compared` | ≥0.90 | Did the model pick the right function? Names only. |
| `argument_exact_match` | `exact_dict_match / total_tools_compared` using `_deep_equals` | ≥0.75 | Did the arguments match the GT *exactly* — same keys, same string values, no extras? |
| `tool_call_f1` | BFCL-style AST F1: precision/recall over (name, normalised-args) sub-trees | ≥0.85 | More forgiving than `argument_exact_match` — accepts subtree matches and tolerates extra-but-valid arguments. |
| `hallucinated_tool_rate` | `predicted_tools_not_in_schema / total_predicted` | ≤0.03 | Did the model invent function names that aren't in the provided `tool_schemas`? |

### Per-turn vs conversation: why both?

- **Per-turn (`tool_metrics`)** is brittle: if the GT has 4 turns with tool calls and the model produces 3 (or 5), zip-alignment slides everything by one and most turns mismatch. Useful only when turn counts already line up — i.e. the model didn't collapse multi-turn negotiation.
- **Per-conversation (`tool_metrics_conversation`)** ignores turn ordering. As long as the right *set* of (name, args) tuples appeared somewhere, it scores. This is what `weighted_workflow_score` actually reads (via the `max(turn, conv)` rule in `composite_score.py`).

A common pattern: `tool_metrics.tool_call_f1 = 0.10` while `tool_metrics_conversation.tool_call_f1 = 0.45`. That gap is *not* model error — it's structural disagreement between the model's turn-collapsing and zip-based per-turn alignment.

### `argument_exact_match` is harsher than the name suggests

It calls `_deep_equals`, which requires:
- Same key set (no extra keys, no missing keys)
- Same value type for every key
- For strings: byte-exact equality (no case-fold, no whitespace strip)
- For numbers: numeric equality
- For nested dicts/lists: recursive `_deep_equals`

So `{"product": "premium"}` vs `{"product": "premium package"}` is a miss, and `{"customer_id": "12345"}` vs `{"customer_id": "12345", "reason": "upgrade"}` is also a miss (extra key on the prediction side). Frontier models in particular drift on optional-field inclusion. Read this metric alongside `tool_call_f1`, which is more forgiving.

---

## 4. Chain-propagation metrics (`tool_chain_propagation.py`)

Tests whether outputs from earlier tool calls correctly populate inputs of later ones — e.g. `lookup_user(email)` returns `{"user_id": "U123"}` and a later `update_subscription(user_id="U123", ...)` must reuse that exact value.

| Metric | Formula | Target | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| `chain_propagation_accuracy` | `correctly_propagated_chains / total_chains_in_GT` | ≥0.70 | Per-chain binary success. |
| `per_depth_accuracy` | Same, broken down by chain depth (2, 3, 4, …) | — | Useful for L4/L5 diagnostics. |
| `total_chains` | Count of GT chains in the run | — | If 0, the dataset has no multi-tool chains; the metric is N/A. |

`total_chains == 0` is normal for L1 (single-tool calls only) and means `chain_propagation_accuracy` should be ignored, not interpreted as failure.

---

## 5. Composite metrics (`composite_score.py`)

### `weighted_workflow_score` — the headline number

```
0.4 × max(state_transition_accuracy, state_sequence_accuracy)
+ 0.4 × max(tool_metrics.tool_call_f1, tool_metrics_conversation.tool_call_f1)
+ 0.2 × task_completion_rate
```

Range: [0, 1]. Target: ≥0.75 for a fine-tuned specialist.

This metric is **continuous** (degrades smoothly) and **lenient** (uses the better of the strict and lenient state/tool metrics). Use this for ranking across models and complexity levels.

### `full_workflow_success` — strict perfection check

A conversation counts as successful if and only if **all four** hold:
1. Every per-turn state transition exactly matches GT (`state_transition_accuracy == 1.0`)
2. Zero invalid transitions for the entire conversation
3. Reached a GT terminal state
4. Every tool-call turn scores `compute_ast_f1 == 1.0`

The result is the fraction of conversations that pass all four gates.

This is a **binary, all-or-nothing** metric. A 19-turn conversation that's 95% correct scores 0, the same as one that's 0% correct. **Do not use this as a ranking metric** — use it as a diagnostic answering "did the eval pipeline even produce a perfect trajectory once?" (For example, on Gemini-3.1-Flash-Lite-Preview run 2026-05-06, L1 hits 0.625 while L2–L5 are all 0 — confirming the metric works on simple flows but is mathematically pinned to zero on multi-turn complexity.)

The 0.55 target in `.claude/rules/05-eval.md` is set for **fine-tuned models only**. Treat `full_workflow_success` for pre-trained frontier as a perfection canary, not a quality measure.

---

## 6. Latency

| Metric | Definition |
|---|---|
| `latency_per_turn_avg_ms` | Mean wall-clock per assistant turn (request → final SSE chunk). |
| `latency_per_turn_median_ms` | Median across turns. Resilient to cold-start / streaming stalls. |
| `ttft_avg_ms` | Mean time to first token of the streamed response. |

Use median latency for cross-model comparison; mean is skewed by the first turn's TLS handshake and any retries. TTFT is a good proxy for perceived responsiveness in conversational UX.

---

## 7. How to read a result file

```bash
jq '.complexity_level, .num_samples, .metrics.weighted_workflow_score' \
  results/exp_a/<MODEL>_frontier_l3.json
```

Recommended interpretation order:

1. **`weighted_workflow_score`** — headline. If this is below 0.5 across L1, the model probably can't follow the format; below 0.5 only at L4–L5 means it scales poorly.
2. **`tool_metrics_conversation.tool_call_f1`** — the tool capability number. If this is high (>0.6) but per-turn is low (<0.3), the model's getting the right calls but in a different turn ordering than GT. That's usually a harness problem, not a model problem.
3. **`state_metrics.state_sequence_accuracy`** — does the model follow the right *shape*? If 1.0, it knows the workflow.
4. **`state_metrics.state_transition_accuracy`** — strict turn-level correctness. Read together with `state_sequence_accuracy`. A wide gap (say 0.30 / 0.95) means the model takes detours but arrives correctly.
5. **`task_completion_rate`** — lenient terminal-reached signal.
6. **`recovery_rate`** — only meaningful if there are tool errors in the trajectory; ~20% of GT tool calls return error payloads.
7. **`full_workflow_success`** — diagnostic only. Expect 0 on anything beyond L1.
8. **`chain_propagation_accuracy`** — only meaningful for L3+ where multi-tool chains exist. L1 always shows 0 because `total_chains == 0`.

---

## 8. Known caveats and structural pitfalls

These are *harness*-level issues that affect metric values without reflecting model quality. Be aware of them when reading numbers.

### `tool_metrics` (per-turn) inflates pessimism on multi-turn

`zip(pred.messages, gt.messages)` is the alignment strategy. When the model collapses or expands the conversation by even one turn, every subsequent comparison slides off-by-one. This is why `tool_metrics_conversation` is the metric that drives the composite score.

### `argument_exact_match` punishes paraphrase

`_deep_equals` requires byte-exact string equality. Real-world models pass `"premium"` when GT expects `"premium package"`, or include `"reason": "upgrade"` as an extra optional argument the GT didn't list. Both are "wrong" by this metric. Use `tool_call_f1` (subtree match) for the gentler reading.

### `consistency_pass5` is misnamed

The metric requires *all* `stochastic_trials` to agree on the terminal state. With `stochastic_trials=2` (the cost-controlled default for frontier models), it's pass^2, not pass^5. The name is historical from when the spec called for 5 trials.

### `chain_propagation_accuracy = 0` does not always mean failure

If `total_chains == 0` (e.g. L1 with single-tool conversations), the metric returns 0 because of the `max(total_chains, 1)` denominator. Always cross-reference `total_chains > 0` before interpreting this as a model deficiency.

### `state_transition_accuracy` is *micro*-averaged, not per-conversation

Total transitions across all conversations divided by total correct. A few long-and-bad conversations dominate over many short-and-good ones. If you want "what fraction of conversations had a perfect state trace?" use `state_sequence_accuracy` (which is per-conversation then macro-averaged).

### Latency depends heavily on the serving path

`vllm` runs go through a self-hosted server with controlled cold-start; `bifrost`/frontier runs go over the open internet to a third-party API. Don't compare ms-for-ms across engines — use TTFT to reason about perceived latency rather than raw wall-clock per turn.

---

## 9. Per-level expectations (calibration)

For a competent pre-trained 3B–35B model **without fine-tuning**, expect the headline `weighted_workflow_score` to roughly degrade as:

| Level | Domain | Tools / chains | Realistic frontier range |
|---|---|---|---|
| L1 | faq_lookup, single-tool | 1 tool / 0 chains | 0.70–0.85 |
| L2 | order_status_cancel | 2 tools / depth 1 | 0.55–0.75 |
| L3 | booking_payment | 4 tools / depth 2 | 0.40–0.60 |
| L4 | it_troubleshoot | 6 tools / depth 3 | 0.35–0.55 |
| L5 | multi_dept_workflow | 7 tools / depth 4 | 0.25–0.45 |

A fine-tuned specialist trained against the corresponding SFT corpus is expected to clear ≥0.75 on its target level; the 0.85 / 0.85 / 0.75 component targets in `.claude/rules/05-eval.md` are calibrated for that case.

If a frontier model lands well below the L1 range (say `weighted_workflow_score < 0.50` on L1), that's almost always either (a) a wrong `tool_call_parser` setting in the model config, (b) the model emitting tool calls in a non-OpenAI format the harness fails to parse, or (c) provider-side errors (HTTP 400s, timeouts) that the harness recovers from with empty turns. Inspect the run log for `http_error_during_call` warnings before concluding the model is bad.

---

## 10. Quick reference — all metrics in one table

| Group | Metric | Type | Target | Read first? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Composite | `weighted_workflow_score` | continuous | ≥0.75 | yes |
| Composite | `full_workflow_success` | binary per-conv | (diagnostic) | no — diagnostic only |
| State | `state_transition_accuracy` | continuous (turn) | ≥0.85 | secondary |
| State | `state_sequence_accuracy` | continuous (conv) | — | yes |
| State | `task_completion_rate` | continuous (conv) | ≥0.70 | yes |
| State | `invalid_transition_rate` | continuous | ≤0.05 | yes |
| State | `recovery_rate` | continuous | ≥0.60 | only if errors present |
| State | `consistency_pass5` | continuous (conv) | ≥0.40 | for stability check |
| Tool (turn) | `tool_call_f1` | continuous | ≥0.85 | secondary |
| Tool (turn) | `argument_exact_match` | continuous | ≥0.75 | strict-only diagnostic |
| Tool (turn) | `tool_name_accuracy` | continuous | ≥0.90 | yes |
| Tool (turn) | `hallucinated_tool_rate` | continuous | ≤0.03 | yes |
| Tool (conv) | `tool_call_f1` | continuous | ≥0.85 | yes — drives composite |
| Tool (conv) | other fields | same as turn | — | secondary |
| Chain | `chain_propagation_accuracy` | continuous | ≥0.70 | only for L3+ |
| Latency | `latency_per_turn_median_ms` | ms | ≤2000 (L1–L3), ≤5000 (L4–L5) | yes |
| Latency | `ttft_avg_ms` | ms | — | yes |

# Phase 1 / Task A Metrics — Definitions and Interpretation

This document describes every metric computed by `eval/agent_benchmark.py` for the workflow-orchestrator benchmark, what each one actually measures, how it can fail, and how to read a result file.

It mirrors the code at `src/llm_workflow_agents/eval/{state_accuracy,tool_call_f1,tool_chain_propagation,composite_score}.py` as of the commits that added per-level evaluation. Targets in the tables are aspirational thresholds for a fully fine-tuned specialist (Phase 2 output), not for pre-trained frontier models.

---

## 1. Where the numbers come from

A benchmark run produces one JSON file under `results/exp_a/` with this shape:

```json
{
  "model": "...",
  "engine": "vllm" | "bifrost",
  "complexity_level": "L1" | ... | "L5" | "mixed",
  "num_samples": 40,
  "stochastic_trials": 2,
  "metrics": {
    "weighted_workflow_score": 0.553,
    "full_workflow_success":   0.000,
    "state_metrics":           { ... },
    "tool_metrics":            { ... },   // per-turn (strict, zip-aligned)
    "tool_metrics_conversation": { ... }, // per-conversation (lenient, set-aligned)
    "chain_metrics":           { ... },
    "latency_per_turn_avg_ms": 993.5,
    "ttft_avg_ms":             808.0
  }
}
```

The composite scoring is in `eval/composite_score.py`. The component metrics are produced by three modules described below.

---

## 2. State-machine metrics (`state_accuracy.py`)

Computed from the `[STATE: X → Y]` annotations the model emits. The harness replays the conversation turn-by-turn against the ground truth.

| Metric | Formula | Aggregation | Target | What it tells you |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `state_transition_accuracy` | `correct_transitions / total_transitions` | per-turn, micro-averaged across all turns of all conversations | ≥0.85 | Strict per-turn correctness. Penalises any deviation, including legal alternative paths. |
| `state_sequence_accuracy` | LCS-recall of the *visited-state* sequence against GT | per-conversation, then averaged | (none, reporting) | Lenient version. Collapses self-loops, ignores ordering of equivalent transitions. Counts a conversation as correct if it covers the GT states in the right order, even if turn counts differ. |
| `task_completion_rate` | `1 if pred_terminal in gt_terminals else 0` | per-conversation, then averaged | ≥0.70 | Did the model end the conversation in *any* terminal state listed in the workflow graph? |
| `invalid_transition_rate` | `transitions_not_in_graph / total_transitions` | micro-averaged | ≤0.05 | Did the model invent edges that don't exist in the GT state machine? |
| `recovery_rate` | `recovered_errors / total_errors_seen` | micro-averaged | ≥0.60 | When a tool returned an error, did the model re-prompt / retry / branch correctly instead of cascading the failure? |
| `consistency_pass5` | `1 if all stochastic trials reached the same terminal else 0` | per-conversation, then averaged | ≥0.40 | Stability under temperature=0.7. Note: name says "5" but `stochastic_trials` is configurable; with `stochastic_trials=2` this is effectively pass^2. |

### Interpretation guide

- **`state_transition_accuracy` is the strictest metric in this group.** It zeros out on any single bad turn. It's normal for it to be much lower than `state_sequence_accuracy` — a 0.40 / 0.85 split means "the model gets the shape right but takes detours."
- **`state_sequence_accuracy`** is the more useful "did the model basically do the right thing?" signal.
- **`recovery_rate` is undefined when `total_errors_seen == 0`.** The code returns 0.0 in that case (`max(total_errors, 1)` denominator) — it does *not* mean the model failed; it means nothing went wrong to recover from. Read it together with the synthetic ~20% tool-error rate from data generation: if the recovery_rate is ~0 across many samples, the model is genuinely cascading failures.
- **`task_completion_rate` is lenient by design** — it accepts any GT-listed terminal. If a workflow has both `RESOLVED` and `ESCALATE_SUPERVISOR` as terminals, ending in either counts.

---

## 3. Tool-call metrics (`tool_call_f1.py`)

Computed by parsing `<tool_call>{JSON}</tool_call>` from `content` (and structured `tool_calls` if the API returned them — they get appended as text tags by the streaming wrapper). Two separate aggregations are computed per run:

- **`tool_metrics`** — strict per-turn alignment. `zip(pred.messages, gt.messages)` then compare.
- **`tool_metrics_conversation`** — set-based per-conversation. Collect every tool call in the trajectory and compare against the GT bag.

Both produce the same dataclass:

| Metric | Formula | Target | What it tells you |
|---|---|---|---|
| `tool_name_accuracy` | `correct_tool_names / total_tools_compared` | ≥0.90 | Did the model pick the right function? Names only. |
| `argument_exact_match` | `exact_dict_match / total_tools_compared` using `_deep_equals` | ≥0.75 | Did the arguments match the GT *exactly* — same keys, same string values, no extras? |
| `tool_call_f1` | BFCL-style AST F1: precision/recall over (name, normalised-args) sub-trees | ≥0.85 | More forgiving than `argument_exact_match` — accepts subtree matches and tolerates extra-but-valid arguments. |
| `hallucinated_tool_rate` | `predicted_tools_not_in_schema / total_predicted` | ≤0.03 | Did the model invent function names that aren't in the provided `tool_schemas`? |

Frontier models in particular drift on optional-field inclusion. Read this metric alongside `tool_call_f1`, which is more forgiving.

---

Tests whether outputs from earlier tool calls correctly populate inputs of later ones — e.g. `lookup_user(email)` returns `{"user_id": "U123"}` and a later `update_subscription(user_id="U123", ...)` must reuse that exact value.

| Tool (turn) | `argument_exact_match` | continuous | ≥0.75 | strict-only diagnostic |

| Tool (turn) | `hallucinated_tool_rate` | continuous | ≤0.03 | yes |
| Tool (conv) | `tool_call_f1` | continuous | ≥0.85 | yes — drives composite |
| Tool (conv) | other fields | same as turn | — | secondary |
| Chain | `chain_propagation_accuracy` | continuous | ≥0.70 | only for L3+ |
| Latency | `latency_per_turn_median_ms` | ms | ≤2000 (L1–L3), ≤5000 (L4–L5) | yes |
| Latency | `ttft_avg_ms` | ms | — | yes |

lastPrompt: Document about metrics, their interpretation and significance.

content.text:
Document written to `docs/metrics.md`. Ten sections:

1. **Where the numbers come from** — result-JSON shape and where each module contributes.
2. **State-machine metrics** — every field in `StateMachineMetrics` with formula, aggregation level, target, and what it tells you.
3. **Tool-call metrics** — both per-turn and conversation aggregations, with the explicit caveat that `argument_exact_match` is byte-exact and `_deep_equals` rejects extra optional args (the "premium" vs "premium package" trap).
4. **Chain-propagation metrics** — including the `total_chains == 0 → ignore the score` rule for L1.
5. **Composite metrics** — the `weighted_workflow_score` formula explicitly written out, and a clear stance that `full_workflow_success` is a diagnostic, not a ranking metric.
6. **Latency** — when to use median vs mean.
7. **How to read a result file** — recommended interpretation order so a reader doesn't focus on the wrong number.
8. **Known caveats** — the structural ones we hit during the Gemini run: zip-alignment, paraphrase punishment, mis-named `consistency_pass5`, micro-averaging, engine-dependent latency.
9. **Per-level expectations** — calibration table for what a non-fine-tuned frontier model should reasonably score per level, plus a debugging triage list when scores are anomalously low.
10. **Quick reference** — single table with every metric, target, and "read first?" priority.

The targets quoted match `.claude/rules/05-eval.md` and the per-level expected ranges are derived from the gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview run we just completed (L1=0.80, L2=0.71, L3=0.53, L4=0.56, L5=0.38).

content: Goal: benchmark Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite on Task A across L1–L5 via BiFrost. Per-level results are in and the metrics doc is written. Next: decide whether to soften `full_workflow_success` gates or rerun on a stronger frontier model.

input.command:
git add scripts/run_exp_a_single.sh scripts/run_exp_a_per_level.sh src/llm_workflow_agents/eval/agent_benchmark.py docs/metrics.md && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
feat(eval): per-level Phase-1 runs + harness fixes for frontier models

- agent_benchmark.py:
  - walk every GT user/tool turn even after the model emits TERMINAL
    so multi-turn negotiations stop getting their late tool calls truncated
    out of per-turn alignment
  - synthesize OpenAI-format `tool_calls` (with UUID ids) when the model
    emits inline `<tool_call>` text instead of structured tool_calls,
    keeping conversations well-formed past the first tool turn
  - downgrade past `tool_calls` / `role=tool` messages to plain text on
    the way out for `engine=bifrost`, sidestepping Gemini-3's required
    `thought_signature` field that BiFrost's OpenAI translation strips
  - recover from HTTPError 4xx by logging the response body and returning
    an empty turn instead of aborting a 600-conversation run on a single
    transient or per-conversation provider rejection
  - accept a single JSONL file (not just a directory) on `--data`
  - record `complexity_level` in result JSON and `benchmark_start` log

- run_exp_a_single.sh:
  - new `--level L1..L5` flag resolves DATA_DIR to the matching JSONL
    and tags result filename with `_lN` so per-level runs don't collide

- run_exp_a_per_level.sh: new wrapper that loops L1..L5, tees per-level
  log files, and prints a one-line `weighted_workflow_score` per level

- docs/metrics.md: explain every field in the result JSON, how to read
  them in order, the structural caveats (zip-alignment, byte-exact
  argument match, etc.), and per-level calibration ranges so future
  readers don't misinterpret `full_workflow_success=0` as model failure

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" && git status
